---
title: "Introduction"
---

Tiny DSA is a stylized debt-sustainability tool for projecting a country's general-government debt-to-GDP ratio over a five-year horizon under a baseline macroeconomic and fiscal path and one configurable shock.[^1] It is intentionally minimal. The model implements a single public-debt accounting identity, recursed annually for five periods. It does not reproduce the indicator-and-threshold structure of operational debt-sustainability frameworks.

This Python package reimplements the illustrative Tiny-DSA Excel workbook for use in scripts, notebooks, and generated documentation. It was produced using a combination of programmatic extraction, machine translation, and AI, while preserving the workbook's intended calculations and documentation conventions at a high level.

Install the package directly from the project repository with `uv`:

```bash
uv pip install "git+https://github.com/Teal-Insights/py-tiny-dsa"
```

The intended reader is a domain economist familiar with the standard public-debt accounting identity, who needs to understand what the package computes, what its inputs and outputs mean, and where its simplifications depart from operational practice. The debt ratio and the primary balance are expressed as percentages of GDP. Real GDP growth and the real interest rate are expressed in percent per annum. Tiny DSA projects in real terms throughout.

Tiny DSA deliberately omits several components that matter in operational debt-sustainability analysis: the contribution of inflation to the debt ratio; exchange-rate dynamics and the currency decomposition of debt; residual financing terms for stock-flow adjustments, contingent-liability calls, and other below-the-line operations; and any endogenous response of the primary balance to the macroeconomic environment.[^2] The fiscal stance is taken as exogenous, the snowball factor is computed in real terms, and shocks are step changes to a single parameter from a configurable shock year onward. These omissions keep the model small enough to be traceable end to end.

This documentation explains how to use the Python package and how to interpret its outputs. The guide is organized around the same concepts as the original workbook guide: a functional overview of the model interface, illustrative scenarios using a fictional country, Borvelia, and a methodology discussion covering the debt-dynamics identity, shock specification, output checks, and limitations.

[^1]: Tiny-DSA is a methodological artifact prepared by Teal Insights. It is not intended for policy use, is not validated against country-specific data, and is not endorsed by the IMF, the World Bank, or any other institution. The modeling choices follow the standard public-debt accounting literature; the documentation conventions follow the IMF/World Bank LIC-DSF and IMF FAD Q-CRAFT guidance documents.

[^2]: For the corresponding treatment in operational frameworks, see International Monetary Fund and World Bank (2018), Sections II.B–II.D and Appendix III, and the discussion in Escolano (2010).

For correctness evidence, see [Excel parity validation](03-excel-parity-validation.qmd), which summarizes the exported-library differential test against the source workbook.
